spec_match.rationale: Page exercises the concept interactively but stops short of touching the real API surface — the underlying spec behaviour is simulated rather than invoked.

interactive.rationale: There is one interactive control and a script, but the surface is thin — closer to a probe than a full hands-on demo.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Concept page lede restates and delivers on the framing used in the feature index card.

edge_cases.rationale: Some unsupported / error handling is wired up, but several spec edge cases (boundary inputs, alternate modes, error states) are not surfaced.

references.rationale: Page cites the required chromestatus.com/feature/6576566521561088 self-heal link (CLAUDE.md rule 3).

design_system.rationale: Uses CSS variables throughout, no raw hex of note, and button overrides keep background + color paired (CLAUDE.md rules 5 and 6).

openQuestions.title: Add a real interactive control surface
openQuestions.detail: The concept page currently reads more like a write-up than a hands-on demo. Add a control that drives the underlying API (or a faithful simulation) and surfaces the live result on the page — buttons, sliders, selects, or a try-it form. CLAUDE.md rule 7 explicitly bans static code cards as concept pages.

openQuestions.title: Exercise more of the Remove auto-detection of ISO-2022-JP charset in HTML spec surface
openQuestions.detail: The spec (see chromestatus) covers more than the page demonstrates. Walk back through the spec's exposed methods, properties, or modes and add controls for the ones that materially change behaviour. Today the page only hints at a subset.

openQuestions.title: Demonstrate failure and edge-case branches
openQuestions.detail: Add visible branches for the unsupported / unavailable / error / boundary inputs paths. For built-in AI APIs, that means 'downloadable', 'downloading', 'unavailable' availability states; for HTTP features, error responses and CSP blocks; for CSS features, zero / extreme / inherited values. Today only the happy path is reachable from the UI.

openQuestions.title: Mirror the spec's stated motivation in the demo
openQuestions.detail: ChromeStatus motivation reads: 'There are known[1] security issues around charset auto-detection for ISO-2022-JP. The use counter[2] shows that the auto-detection of ISO-2022-JP charset only happens around 0.000002% of page load. Given that usage is very low, and Safari d...'. The demo should make that motivation visible — show the problem the spec is solving (before/after, with-without, attacker view) so a reader leaves understanding why the feature ships, not just what it does.

summary: 'Encoding Detection' delivers the core idea but leaves spec edges, blurb framing, or design-system polish on the table. The open questions list captures the rough edges worth closing in the next pass.
